_comment: Organ-specific weight change thresholds for regulatory toxicology assessment. Sources: FDA reviewer practice, Hood (2006), Hall (2012), Capen (1997), ECETOC TRA, Choi (2011), Houle (2014).

_schema.variation_ceiling_pct: Noise ceiling: below this % change, the effect is trivially small and indistinguishable from normal variation. Named 'ceiling' because it is the upper bound of the noise band. Example: liver ceiling=5% means <5% change is noise.
_schema.adverse_floor_pct: Adverse floor: at or above this % change, the effect is biologically meaningful. Named 'floor' because it is the lower bound of the adverse range. Example: liver floor=10% means >=10% change is adverse (if statistically significant).
_schema.strong_adverse_pct: At or above this, the finding is clearly adverse regardless of context
_schema.adaptive_requires: Optional block: conditions required to claim 'adaptive' for this organ
_schema.special_flags: Optional array: organ-specific caveats the engine should annotate
_schema.cross_organ_link: Optional: mechanistic link to another organ's findings
_schema.species_specific: True when thresholds vary by species. _resolve_value() handles dict lookup.
_schema.cv_reference: Documentation only -- published CVs justifying the threshold choices. Not consumed by code.

LIVER.notes: Liver is the most studied organ for adaptive vs adverse distinction. Hall 2012 defined the LB panel gate. Dog thresholds wider due to higher CVs (Choi 2011: M=15%, F=21%).
LIVER.dog_adaptive_notes: ALP elevation without bilirubin elevation should not preclude adaptive classification in dogs (C-ALP confound). ALT is the primary liver injury discriminator.

KIDNEY.notes: Kidney weight changes >=10% are concerning. Male rat a2u-globulin nephropathy is not human-relevant. Dog uses single wider female-driven threshold (Choi 2011: M CV=12%, F CV=18%); 15% ceiling is conservative for males but defensible.

HEART.notes: Heart has low normal variation (CV ~5-8% rodent). Dog heart CV is higher (Choi 2011: M=10%, F=13%).

BRAIN.notes: Brain weight is tightly regulated. Rat/mouse: any statistically significant change is biologically meaningful (any_significant policy). Dog brain CV ~5% (Choi 2011), so dog uses 5% floor instead of any_significant.

ADRENAL.notes: Adrenal weight is highly variable, especially in mice and dogs. Species-specific thresholds prevent false positives. Dog adrenal CV ~20% (Choi 2011).

THYROID.notes: Thyroid weight changes often secondary to liver enzyme induction (liver-thyroid axis). Capen 1997. Dog thyroid CV ~15% (Choi 2011).

SPLEEN.notes: Spleen weight is naturally variable. Large changes may indicate immune response or stress. Dog spleen CV ~20% (Choi 2011).

THYMUS.notes: Thymus weight is age-sensitive and stress-responsive. Involution is normal in older animals. Dog thymus CV ~25% (Choi 2011).

TESTES.notes: Testes weight is tightly regulated in rodents. Dog testes CV ~15% (Choi 2011). Changes >=15% are concerning for reproductive toxicity.

EPIDIDYMIDES.notes: Epididymal weight changes often parallel testicular effects. Dog epididymis CV ~12% (Choi 2011).

OVARIES.notes: Ovarian weight is highly variable due to estrous cycle stage. Higher thresholds prevent false positives. No dog-specific threshold -- ANCOVA-only for dogs (CV >60%).

UTERUS.notes: Uterine weight is the most variable organ weight due to estrous cycle. Very high thresholds required. No dog-specific threshold -- ANCOVA-only for dogs (CV >60%).

LUNGS.notes: Lung weight changes may indicate edema, inflammation, or fibrosis. Residual blood can confound. NHP: perfusion/inflation variability makes quantitative screening unreliable.

PANCREAS.notes: High variability organ (colony CV ~27-30% for NHP). Not always weighed in NHP tox studies. NHP: qualitative only.
